data.title: Real estate stocks surge after Powell signals rate change ahead
data.description:
Stocks of real estate brokerages, homebuildings, and mortgage lenders soared late Friday morning after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell signaled that an interest rate cut could be on the horizon.

In a widely anticipated speech, the central bank head said, "with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

Though, not directly tied to the federal funds rate, mortgage rates generally move with the Fed's rate adjustments. A rate cut makes borrowing cheaper for potential homebuyers. As of Thursday, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage averaged 6.58%, up from 6.46% a year ago.

If the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) holds onto its 2.0% gain for the day, it would be its biggest increase since April 14, 2025. The Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) rose 1.72%, its largest jump since May 12, should the gain hold.

Anywhere Real Estate (NYSE:HOUS) climbed 7.0%, Re/Max Holdings (RMAX) surged 8.1%, Zillow Group (NASDAQ:Z) rose 5.9%, eXp World Holdings (EXPI) grew 6.6%. Opendoor Technologies (NASDAQ:OPEN), the real estate app that's gained meme-stock status, soared 19%.

Rocket Companies (NYSE:RKT), the parent of Rocket Mortgages and the Redfin real estate platform, advanced 9.1%. The company also agreed to buy mortgage servicer Mr. Cooper Group (COOP), which jumped 7.8%. loanDepot (LDI) gained 8.2%, and LendingTree (TREE) climbed 7.8%. PennyMac Financial Services (PFSI), which originates and services mortgages, increased 5.9%.

Mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC) rose 8.0%.

data.title: 13 out of 15 companies beat analysts’ estimates this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street gained ground on Friday as investors reacted positively to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole.

Across sectors, all 11 S&P groups were trading higher, with Consumer Discretionary and Real Estate leading the advance. 

Powell's dovish Jackson Hole comments suggested that the Federal Reserve is ready to cut interest rates in September, which is just what investors were hoping to hear, given the recent slowdown in the labor market. 

Analysts believe that while there is still one more employment report before the September meeting, it's clear the Fed has enough data under its belt to justify a cut. 

Earnings Roundup: 

Out of the 15 S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, 13 of them surpassed expectations for both top-line and bottom-line numbers. 

Let’s take a look at the quarterly performance of some of the companies that reported earnings this week. 

Home Depot (HD) reported revenue rose 4.8% year-over-year to $45.3 billion during the company's second quarter. Comparable sales increased 1.0% for the quarter that ended on August 3 to miss the consensus expectation for an increase of 1.4%. 

During the quarter, foreign exchange rates negatively impacted total company comparable sales by approximately 40 basis points. 

Target's (TGT) second quarter results beat analysts’ modest expectations with a profit of $2.05 per share on $25.21B in revenue, both of which were down from a year ago. 

Gross margin narrowed 100 basis points year-over-year to 29%, reflecting deeper markdowns, purchase order cancellation costs, and pressure from category mix. 

Walmart (WMT) reported a growth of 4.6% in comparable sales in the U.S. to top the consensus estimate of 4.1%. E-commerce sales rose 26% during the quarter and contributed 350 basis points to comparable sales.

Looking ahead, Walmart guided for net sales to grow between 3.75% to 4.75% for the fiscal year, up from its previous expectations of 3% to 4%. The retail giant said it expects Q3 EPS of $0.58 to $0.60 and full-year EPS of $2.52 to $2.62. The company mentioned tariffs as a general risk factor.

Intuit (INTU) projected Q1 and FY2026 revenue growth will slow from FY2025 rates. Meanwhile, fiscal Q4 earnings and revenue beat the Wall Street consensus estimates.

The company expects FY2026 revenue of $20.997B-$21.186B, representing growth of ~12%-13% Y/Y. Full-year non-GAAP EPS is expected to be $22.98-$23.18, compared with the average analyst estimate of $22.99.

Back-office software provider Workday (WDAY) reported fiscal second-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations.

For the period ending July 31, Workday said it earned an adjusted $2.21 per share as revenue rose 13% year-over-year to $2.35B. Analysts were expecting adjusted earnings of $2.11 per share on $2.34B in revenue.

Cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks (PANW) posted fiscal fourth-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations. For the period ending July 31, the firm earned an adjusted $0.95 per share on $2.5B in revenue, up 16% year-over-year.

A consensus of analysts expected the company to earn $0.89 per share on $2.5B in revenue.

Earnings next week:

Several companies are set to report earnings next week, such as Heico (HEI) and Semtech (SMTC) on Monday, and Bank of Montreal (BMO), MongoDB (MDB), and Okta (OKTA) on Tuesday. Meanwhile big names such as NVIDIA (NVDA), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Snowflake (SNOW), and HP (HPQ) will report earnings on Wednesday. 

Thursday will see earnings reports from Alibaba (BABA), Dell (DELL), Dollar General (DG), Li Auto (LI), Ulta Beauty (ULTA), and Best Buy (BBY), with Frontline (FRO), MOGU (MOGU), and Immuron (IMRN) reporting on Friday.

data.title: Enterprise software stocks follow broader market and jump following Powell speech
data.description:
Enterprise software stocks, along with most of the market, jumped following Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech on Friday morning in Jackson Hole, Wyo.

Powell indicated the central bank is possibly considering another rate cut in September.

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), which suffered from multiple days of share price decline, promptly reversed course, climbing 4% by noon trading. Zoom (ZM) was leading the pack with a 10% surge as the speech came on top of its latest positive financial results.

ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) edged up 1.3%, Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) ticked up 1.5%, while Atlassian (NASDAQ:TEAM) and Appian (APPN) both increased 3.4%. Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) had inched up 0.5%, and SAP (NYSE:SAP) was up 1%.

The software giant Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) added 0.7%. Monday.com (NASDAQ:MNDY) had climbed 3% as it attempted to reverse a 36% plunge that has accrued over the past month. Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) perked up 1.9%.

Cybersecurity software stocks were also riding the wave. CyberArk (CYBR) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) both climbed 1%, CrowdStrike (CRWD) increased 2%, and Fortinet (FTNT) jumped 3%.

The fed chair was relatively upbeat on the state of the U.S. economy, but he did note some potential issues stemming from tariffs and immigration policy. 

"Changes in trade and immigration policies are affecting both demand and supply," Powell said. "In this environment, distinguishing cyclical developments from trend, or structural, developments is difficult. This distinction is critical because monetary policy can work to stabilize cyclical fluctuations but can do little to alter structural changes."

Although payroll job growth was slower than expected, "it does not appear that the slowdown in job growth has opened up a large margin of slack in the labor market—an outcome we want to avoid," he said. "The unemployment rate, while edging up in July, stands at a historically low level of 4.2% and has been broadly stable over the past year."

"The effects of tariffs on consumer prices are now clearly visible," Powell said. "We expect those effects to accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts. The question that matters for monetary policy is whether these price increases are likely to materially raise the risk of an ongoing inflation problem. A reasonable base case is that the effects will be relatively short-lived—a one-time shift in the price level."

data.title: GLP-1 drugs such as Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo’s Ozempic linked to lower cancer risk: study
data.description:
GLP-1 receptor agonists such as the popular weight loss/diabetes therapies Mounjaro, developed by Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), and Ozempic, developed by Novo Nordisk (NVO), can reduce cancer risk, according to research findings published on Jama Oncology this week.

After studying electronic health records of more than 80,000 obese patients with no prior cancer history, the researchers noted that GLP-1 users indicated a statistically significant decline in overall cancer risk compared to non-users.

The study was based on EHR data from the health research network, OneFlorida+, representing 43,315 non-GLP-1 users and 43,317 users who were on GLP-1s, including Novo Nordisk’s (OTCPK:NONOF) Ozempic as well as Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Mounjaro from 2014 to 2024.

Their findings suggest that the incidence rate for 14 cancer types, including 13 obesity-associated cancers such as endometrial tumors, stood at 13.6 among thousands of patients using GLP-1s each year compared to 16.4 among non-users.

The study led by Hao Dai, a health data scientist at Indiana University, further indicated that GLP-1 drugs are, however, associated with a slight and nonsignificant increase in risk for kidney cancer.

Given the retrospective nature of the study and the fact that no GLP-1 drugs are indicated for cancer, the researchers called for a longer-term follow-up to evaluate the clinical implications and reasons behind their findings.

data.title: Trump says he'll fire Fed's Cook if she doesn't quit - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump told reporters on Friday that he would fire Lisa Cook from the Federal Reserve Board of Governors if she doesn't resign after she was accused of mortgage fraud.

The president can’t fire a Fed governor over a disagreement on monetary policy, but can “for cause,” such as malfeasance or neglect of duty, the Associated Press said.

Trump has been ramping up his pressure campaign on the central bank for months, as he repeatedly demands that the Fed cut interest rates. But the Supreme Court has shielded the central bank from the president's power to remove personnel at will. Rather, he requires a "good cause" for firing staff, such as misconduct or illegal acts.

The focus on Cook appears to be taking some of the heat off of Chair Jerome Powell, whom Republicans have accused of mismanaging the multibillion-dollar renovation of the Fed headquarters.  Powell's term as chair ends in May, though his term on the Fed board extends to Jan. 31, 2028.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Gold Fields H1 profit climbs to $1B as bullion prices and production surge
data.description:
Gold Fields (NYSE:GFI) +1.1% in Friday's trading after reporting H1 profit that nearly tripled from a year earlier, driven by record bullion prices and increased production.

The miner said H1 net profit rose to $1.02B from $389M for the year-earlier period, with headline earnings jumping to $1.15/share from $0.36/share, in line with expectations of $1.09-$1.21/share.

Gold Fields (NYSE:GFI) said it realized an average gold price of $3,281/oz in H1, up 40% Y/Y, and gold production jumped 24% Y/Y to 1.136M oz.

For Q2, attributable gold equivalent production rose 6.2% Q/Q to 585K oz, in line with forecasts, but all-in sustaining costs wclimbed to $2,054/oz from $1,861/oz in Q1, also in line with estimates.

For the full year, the company backed previous production guidance of 2.25M-2.45M gold equivalent oz, with AISC expected at $1,500-$1,650 oz.

The company declared an interim dividend of 7 rand/share ($0.3948), up from 3 rand/share in H1 2024.

Gold Fields (GFI) CEO Mike Fraser told Reuters that the ramp-up at the new Salares Norte mine in Chile has progressed well after it was slowed by extreme weather last year.

Salares Norte produced 123,600 gold equivalent oz in H1 and is expected to reach commercial levels of production in Q3; the mine is expected to produce 325K-375K oz in 2025 and 550K-580K oz in 2026, its first full year of steady-state production.

data.title: Yields drop in response to Powell speech at Jackson Hole
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields edged lower on Friday as investors moved into government debt following remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell at the Jackson Hole Economic Symposium.

In his speech, Powell acknowledged that the balance of risks facing the economy has shifted, noting that conditions “may warrant adjusting our policy stance.”

Market participants interpreted the comments as a signal that the central bank could begin easing interest rates as soon as September, depending on the flow of economic data.

The response in the bond market was swift. The benchmark U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) declined by 8 basis points to 4.24%, while the more rate-sensitive U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) slipped 10 basis points to 3.68%.

The move reflects heightened demand for Treasuries as investors position for potential monetary easing. Expectations for a rate cut in September climbed significantly in the aftermath of Powell’s remarks as well.

The decline in yields underscores investor confidence that the Fed may soon pivot to a more accommodative stance, with markets increasingly convinced that borrowing costs could begin to ease in the coming months.

See how yields are trading across the entire curve here on Seeking Alpha’s bond page.

Treasury ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), and (NYSEARCA:BIL).

Bond ETFs: (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: Bitcoin, ether spike after Fed's Powell signals rate cut is near
data.description:
Crypto prices surged Friday after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell delivered his strongest signal yet that interest-rate cuts are on the horizon, paring a good portion of this week's losses.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD), the two largest tokens by market cap, jumped 3.6% to $116.5K and +10% to $4.65K, respectively, in the last 24 hours as of 11:08 a.m. ET. Other major coins on the move include Ethena (ENA-USD) +15%, Cronos (CRO-USD) +9%, Arbit Coin (ARB-USD) +8.4%, Aave (AAVE-USD) +8.5% and Cardano (ADA-USD) +7.1%.

Even with Friday's rally, BTC was down nearly 2% on the week, though ETH continued to outperform.

The intraday swings followed Powell's dovish remarks. A shifting balance of risks "may warrant adjusting our policy stance," he said in his widely anticipated address at the Kansas City's Fed Central Bank Symposium at Jackson Hole, Wyoming.

"Crypto remains sensitive to broader risk sentiment and could face renewed swings depending on macro headlines. Still, today’s tone is incrementally more constructive compared to earlier in the week," said David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation.

Powell's speech prompted traders to boost the odds of a rate cut when the policy-setting Federal Open Market Committee gathers on Sept. 16-17. The chance of a September rate cut was at ~71% before the speech and rose to ~93% after it.

Risk asset soared across the board, including crypto. All three of the major U.S. stock indices advanced at least 1% each.

Crypto-focused stocks gapped higher, with Coinbase Global (COIN) +6.8%, Bullish (BLSH) +1.6%, Circle Internet Group (CRCL) +9.4%, Strategy (MSTR) +5.6%, Riot Platforms (RIOT) +7.4%, MARA Holdings (MARA) +6.1%, SharpLink Gaming (SBET) +11% and CleanSpark (CLSK) +5.7%.

data.title: Gap's 'blue sky scenario' off the table as tariff pressures pinch margins - Barclays
data.description:
Tariff pressures and macro uncertainty will likely disrupt Gap’s (NYSE:GAP) momentum, and after six consecutive quarters of “meaningful” operating margin expansion, Barclays’ Adrienne Yih thinks the company is entering a phase of margin compression, leading the analyst to downgrade the retailer to Equal Weight from Overweight and shave 21% off the target price.

We are downgrading shares of GAP from Overweight to Equal Weight as we now believe our previous blue-sky scenario for significant margin and upward EPS revisions driving towards a ~10% [operating margin] by FY26 is off the table, diminished by tariff margin pressure and macro uncertainty. We are moving to the sidelines and wait for a better entry point to reestablish positions in shares of GAP once sustainable margin expansion is more visible,” Yih said in her note to clients.

Yih attributes her downgrade to several developments that could undermine Gap's (NYSE:GAP) financial performance including increased promotional activity at the Gap and Athleta banners, and a greater impact on FY26 operating margins from new tariffs that went into effect August 7. Additionally, the 2011 cotton inflation demonstrated that apparel retailers lacked the ability to pass higher input costs to consumers, suggesting that tariff-induced cost increases would leave the company with few pricing levers.

Barclays now has a $19 price target on Gap (NYSE:GAP) to reflect weaker pricing power, outsized tariff impact, and expectation for softer long-term growth, all of which will stifle upward momentum for the stock price into fiscal year 2026.

Gap (GAP) reports second quarter results on August 28, expected to show an adjusted profit of $0.55 per share on $3.73B in revenue, showing an increase of 1.9% and 0.2% year-over-year, respectively.

After early weakness tied to Barclays’ downgrade, Gap (GAP) shares are in the plus column for a third consecutive day, moving in tandem with a rally in the broader market.

data.title: Chip stocks gain after Fed Chair Powell's speech at Jackson Hole
data.description:
Chip stocks were in the green on Friday after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell signaled the Fed will potentially cut rates in September if data warrants.

Powell delivered the key speech at a central banking symposium in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. It is likely Powell’s last time addressing the marquee event, with his term as Fed chair ending in May.

AI chipmakers Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) rose around 2% each on Friday. Broadcom (AVGO) and Qualcomm (QCOM) jumped roughly 3% each.

Shares of NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), GlobalFoundries (GFS), and STMicroelectronics (STM) surged about 5% each.  Texas Instruments (TXN) and Intel (INTC) jumped nearly 4% each, while Micron Technology (MU) and Marvell Technology (MRVL) each climbed about 3%. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) rose nearly 2%.

Meanwhile, chip equipment makers: Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX) and Dutch company ASML (ASML) each climbed nearly 3%, while KLA (KLAC) rose about 2%.
